questions.question: What ceiling does Hogwild! Inference attack that speculative decoding can't?
options: GPU memory capacity limits

Spec decoding parallelizes WITHIN one sequence (3-5x) but the autoregressive dependency remains the hard ceiling — Hogwild! parallelizes ACROSS sequences: 50k reasoning tokens at 35 tok/s is 24 minutes, and N cooperating workers can divide that work
Tokenizer throughput
Network bandwidth between nodes
questions.explanation: An orthogonal axis of inference parallelism: tokens-within-a-sequence (spec decode) vs whole-sequences-in-cooperation (Hogwild!).

questions.question: What is the core Hogwild! setup?
options: N models voting on the final answer

N instances of the SAME LLM share ONE KV cache — every worker sees every other worker's tokens instantly as part of its own context, with write order determined by arrival time; no per-worker position index
A coordinator assigning sub-tasks
One model with N attention heads
questions.explanation: Unlike voting ensembles, tree-of-thought, or multi-agent frameworks, there is no explicit coordination machinery — the shared cache IS the communication channel.

questions.question: Why does coordination emerge without any fine-tuning?
options: A hidden RL objective trains it online

Reasoning models (QwQ, DeepSeek-R1) already have the abilities: prompted with "you are one of N instances sharing memory, avoid redundant work," they read the cache, notice what's been attempted, and pivot — observed forming plans, calling out other workers' errors, and adapting when plans fail
The cache contains special sync tokens
It doesn't — coordination is hardcoded
questions.explanation: The emergent behavior comes entirely from existing reasoning capability plus shared visibility — "I see worker 2 handled the base case, so I'll take the inductive step."

questions.question: What is the namesake connection to Hogwild! SGD?
options: Both were written by the same lab

Hogwild! SGD (2011) had asynchronous workers writing to a shared PARAMETER vector without locks; Hogwild! Inference has workers writing to a shared KV CACHE — both rely on empirical convergence rather than synchronization guarantees
Both require gradient updates
The name is a random choice
questions.explanation: The same engineering bet twice: drop the locks, accept races, and observe that the process still converges in practice.

questions.question: What determines whether Hogwild! actually speeds up a task?
options: Worker count alone — more is always better
Task-level parallelism p and coordination overhead c: speedup tracks how decomposable the problem is and how much cache-reading redundancy costs — workload-dependent and still experimental as of 2026
GPU clock frequency
The prompt's word count
questions.explanation: A serial proof gains little from N workers; a multi-case analysis gains a lot. The wall-time model as a function of N, p, and c is the lesson's analysis exercise.
